content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 5, 2017

Iman Novin, Principal
Novin Development Corporation
1990 N. California Boulevard, Suite 800
Walnut Creek, CA 94596

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-156

Dear Mr. Novin:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the City of Walnut Creek's North Downtown Specific Plan ("NDSP")?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the NDSP because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your business.

FACTS

You are a Walnut Creek Planning Commissioner and an alternate member of the City's NDSP Advisory Committee. In 2014, the Metropolitan Transportation Commission and the Contra Costa Transportation Authority awarded a grant to the City for the preparation of a specific plan covering the 180-acre North Downtown area, which is located adjacent to the City's Bay Area Rapid Transit ("BART") station and extends to the Iron Horse Trail, Parkside Drive, and Civic Drive. The NDSP area currently includes a diverse mix of land uses, including automobile sales and service, residential, mixed-use (office or residential over ground-floor retail), office, and retail uses.

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-156
Page No. 2

The City formally initiated the NDSP planning process in early 2016. In May of that year, the City Council established the NDSP Advisory Committee to review and provide input on the work performed by City staff and consultants related the NDSP. If ultimately incorporated into an approved NDSP, its Land Use Alternatives could result in designation of additional areas where future development of residential and mixed-use projects would be permitted, and its Circulation Concepts could result in various improvements to the NDSP area's vehicular, pedestrian, and bicycle traffic circulation system.

In your private capacity, you are a Principal at, and the sole proprietor of, Novin Development Corporation, a real estate investment, development, and property management company founded in 2004. Since its founding, Novin Development has successfully acquired and repositioned over $25 million worth of real estate throughout the Bay Area. Novin Development currently owns and manages more than 160 residential units and 76,500 square feet of commercial space, including 23,000 square feet of mixed-use retail. Novin Development maintains a "virtual" office on North California Boulevard within the NDSP area that it rents on a month-to-month basis.

Novin Development also provides consulting and real estate brokerage services. In 2013, you founded Novin Development's consulting practice, which provides real estate acquisition, feasibility, and project-management consulting services. Novin Development's clients include nonprofit and for-profit housing developers seeking to acquire sites that are suitable for affordable housing, market-rate housing, and mixed-use projects.

As part of a targeted marketing strategy, Novin Development uses Geographic Information System technology to identify real-property parcels that might be suitable for development or redevelopment by its clients. These criteria include parcel size, proximity to BART and community amenities, whether the parcels are underdeveloped according to information obtained from the County Assessor, and whether the parcels are designated as housing opportunity sites in the housing element of the applicable general plan.

Based on these criteria, Novin Development sends a large number of computer-generated solicitation letters to the owners of identified real property parcels under your signature, expressing interest in acquiring those properties on behalf of your clients. If a property owner responds, you offer to provide a broker's estimate of value. Next steps may involve negotiation of a Letter of Intent or Purchase and Sale Agreement. If the purchase transaction is consummated, you receive income in the form of brokerage fees. Novin Development has not received any brokerage fees related to the acquisition of real property within the City to date.

During the past 12 months, you have received income for consulting services provided on an hourly basis to your clients. None of this consulting income was related to any potential development site within the City; however, some of your consulting clients may now be interested in developing projects within the City, including within the NDSP area. To that end, Novin Development has sent approximately 40-50 solicitation letters to City property owners, a subset of whom are located within the NDSP area.

On May 15, 2017, Councilmembers and City staff received emails from an owner of property within the NDSP area who stated that he had received a solicitation letter from Novin Development expressing interest in acquiring his property on North Broadway, located within the

File No. A-17-156
Page No. 3

NDSP area. The email also included a copy of another Novin Development solicitation letter
received by the owner of a property on Pine Street, also located within the NDSP area.

The North Broadway property is currently designated for office use, and the Pine Street
property is designated for auto sales and services uses. The NDSP's draft Land Use Alternatives
identifies the North Broadway property for possible re-designation for residential or workforce
housing uses, and the Pine Street property for possible re-designation for a "Maker's Row" district,
a flexible designation that would permit production, distribution, and repair uses.

You estimate that the response rate to Novin Development's solicitation letters is generally
about 5 or 6 responses per 100 letters, with some of those responses indicating no interest.
However, at least one property owner within the NDSP who received a Novin Development
solicitation letter has now responded with a level of interest that would normally cause you to take
the next step of providing a broker's estimate of value (free of charge). Assuming continued interest
by the potential seller, Novin Development may become the broker on a transaction involving the
potential seller's property, or provide other real estate services to the potential seller, and receive
income for the provision of those services.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in
that section. Of those interests, the decisions relating to the NDSP and the facts presented implicate
the following:

• An interest in any business entity in which the official has invested $2,000 or more, or in which
the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) Because you are a Principal at and owner of Novin
Development, you have an interest in Novin Development.

• An interest in any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) "Interests in real property of an individual includes a pro
rata share of interests in real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or
immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater."
(Section 82033.)

Novin Development maintains a "virtual office" on North California Boulevard within the
NDSP area that it rents on a month-to-month basis. However, because Regulation 18233
provides that a periodic tenancy of one month or less is excluded from the Act's definitions of
"interest in real property" and "leasehold interest," you do not have a real-property interest in
that tenancy.

File No. A-17-156
Page No. 4

• An interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more provided or promised to, or
received by, the official within 12 months of the decision. (Section 87103(c).) "Income of an
individual also includes a pro rata share of the any income of any business entity or trust in
which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or
greater." (Section 82030.) You are the sole proprietor of Novin Development. Therefore, you
have a source-of-income interest in any source of income to you or Novin Development
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months of the decision.

• An interest in the official's personal finances or those of an immediate family member. (See
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. However,
Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that if a decision also affects an official's interest in a business
entity, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. Thus, we do not further
analyze financial effects on your "personal finances."

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract
with, the financial interest. (Regulation 18701(a).) Your business interest in Novin Development is
not explicitly involved in the decisions relating to the NDSP.

For an interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, the decision's financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable.
(Regulation 18701(b).)

Regulation 18702.1(b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to the financial effect of
a decision on an official's business interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision: "The
effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably
foreseeable that it would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held business entity."

We believe a prudent person, based on the facts provided, would find it reasonably
foreseeable that the decision would contribute to a change in the value of Novin Development.

• Novin Development, through its solicitation letters to owners of real property within the NDSP,
is actively seeking to facilitate client purchases of real properties within the NDSP area that the
decisions at issue concern. Novin Development has actively positioned itself to take advantage
of zoning changes that may be authorized with the adoption of the NDSP.

• Novin Development provides a wide array of real estate and development services and operates
from inside the NDSP area.

• Novin Development's solicitation letters to those property owners may also attract additional
business from them, given that the NDSP may authorize additional uses of their properties.

File No. A-17-156
Page No. 5

• One property owner within the NDSP who received a Novin Development solicitation letter has already responded with a level of interest that would normally cause you to take next steps in securing that potential client.

Therefore, we conclude that a prudent person would find it reasonably foreseeable that the the decisions relating to the NDSP would contribute to a change in the value of Novin Development by increasing the need for its services. Thus, we conclude that the Act prohibits you from taking part in those decisions because they would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your interest in Novin Development.²

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

_______________

² We do not analyze your financial interests in sources of income because we have already determined that the decisions relating to the NDSP would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your interest in Novin Development.

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the City of 
Walnut Creek's North Downtown Specific Plan ("NDSP")?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the NDSP because those 
decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your business.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the 
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in 
that section. Of those interests, the decisions relating to the NDSP and the facts presented implicate 
the following: 
• 
An interest in any business entity in which the official has invested $2,000 or more, or in which 
the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of 
management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) Because you are a Principal at and owner of Novin 
Development, you have an interest in Novin Development. 
• 
An interest in any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) "Interests in real property of an individual includes a pro 
rata share of interests in real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or 
immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a to-percent interest or greater." 
(Section 82033.) 
Novin Development maintains a "virtual office" on North California Boulevard within the 
NDSP area that it rents on a month-to-month basis. However, because Regulation 18233 
provides that a periodic tenancy of one month or less is excluded from the Act's definitions of 
"interest in real property" and "leasehold interest," you do not have a real-property interest in 
that tenancy.

received by, the official within 12 months of the decision. (Section 87103(c).) "Income of an 
individual also includes a pro rata share of the any income of any business entity or trust in 
which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a IO-percent interest or 
greater." (Section 82030.) You are the sole proprietor of Novin Development. Therefore, you

aggregating $500 or more within 12 months of the decision.

entity, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. Thus, we do not further 
analyze financial effects on your "personal finances." 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the decision involves the issuance, 
renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract 
with, the financial interest. (Regulation 1870l(a).) Your business interest in Navin Development is 
not explicitly involved in the decisions relating to the NDSP. 
For an interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, the decision's financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or

(Regulation 1870l(b).) 
Regulation 18702. l(b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to the financial effect of 
a decision on an official's business interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision: "The 
effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably 
foreseeable that it would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held business entity." 
We believe a prudent person, based on the facts provided, would find it reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision would contribute to a change in the value of Novin Development. 
•

is actively seeking to facilitate client purchases of real properties within the NDSP area that the 
decisions at issue concern. Novin Development has actively positioned itself to take advantage 
of zoning changes that may be authorized with thj adoption of the NDSP. 
• 
Novin Development provides a wide array of real estate and development services and operates 
from inside the NDSP area. 
• 
Novin Development's solicitation letters to those property owners may also attract additional 
business from them, given that the NDSP may authorize additional uses of their properties.  
• 
One property owner within the NDSP who received a Novin Development solicitation letter has 
already responded with a level of interest that would normally cause you to take next steps in 
securing that potential client. 
Therefore, we conclude that a prudent person would find it reasonably foreseeable that the 
the decisions relating to the NDSP would contribute to a change in the value of Novin Development

part in those decisions because they would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the City of 
Walnut Creek's North Downtown Specific Plan ("NDSP")?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the NDSP because those 
decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your business.
